pathophysiology_summary: A primary (first) HSV infection causes herpetic gingivostomatitis: a prodrome of malaise/fever then grouped perioral vesicles with widespread intraoral ulceration, intensely erythematous swollen gingivae, tender submandibular lymphadenopathy and severe odynophagia. The acute danger is dehydration — 18 hours without intake, tachycardia, low-normal BP, dry mucosae and prolonged capillary refill — with risk of AKI, especially in a young adult who has stopped drinking. Management combines systemic antivirals (oral valaciclovir/aciclovir), analgesia (systemic ± topical anaesthetic/'magic mouthwash' to enable drinking) and active rehydration, with a low threshold for IV fluids. Discriminators: prominent gingival involvement and lip vesicles/haemorrhagic crusting favour primary HSV over hand-foot-mouth disease (palm/sole lesions, less gingival) and over aphthous stomatitis (no vesicles/gingivitis/fever); the absence of skin target lesions, ocular/genital involvement and a drug trigger argues against SJS. The core telehealth skill is recognising dehydration as the emergency and arranging urgent in-person hydration/assessment.

differential.evidence_basis: Lip vesicles + widespread gingivostomatitis + fever in a first episode.
Oral ulcers but with palm/sole lesions and less gingival involvement.
Severe mucosal disease must be screened for SJS (skin targets, multi-mucosal, drug trigger).
Recurrent mouth ulcers but without lip vesicles, gingivitis or fever.
Inability to drink for 18h is the immediate danger.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Diagnose primary HSV gingivostomatitis, screen for SJS, and treat the dehydration as the emergency (antivirals + analgesia + hydration).

differential.evidence_basis: Classic gingivostomatitis; SJS features negative; dehydration present.
No skin targets, no multi-mucosal involvement, no drug trigger — remains a safety-net.
Working diagnosis: systemic antivirals + analgesia/topical anaesthetic + urgent rehydration/assessment.

red_flags.description: Inability to swallow fluids for ~18h with dehydration (tachycardia, dry mucosae, reduced urine output)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Dehydration risks AKI and decompensation; needs urgent rehydration and analgesia to enable drinking.

red_flags.description: SJS/TEN features (skin target lesions, multi-mucosal involvement, drug trigger, skin detachment)
red_flags.clinical_significance: SJS/TEN is life-threatening; excluded here but must be screened.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Focusing on the ulcers/antivirals and overlooking that inability to drink is the acute danger.
Not providing systemic/topical analgesia so the patient can rehydrate.
Not screening severe mucosal disease for SJS.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Start systemic antivirals and analgesia (± topical anaesthetic) and arrange urgent in-person assessment for hydration; ED for IV fluids if unable to rehydrate or AKI features.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Prescribing antivirals and sending home without addressing the dehydration/inability to drink.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Primary herpetic gingivostomatitis: oral antivirals (aciclovir/valaciclovir), analgesia including topical anaesthetics, and attention to hydration; admit for IV fluids if intake cannot be maintained.
